board.verdict: The Bürgenstock session on June 22 produced the first concrete architecture of the US-Iran nuclear track: a High-Level Committee with Vance and Ghalibaf reporting to political oversight, technical working groups on nuclear issues and sanctions, a Lebanon deconfliction cell with Beirut in the room, and a Hormuz communication line. Pakistan, Qatar, and Switzerland ran the mediation; no European power had a seat. As of June 23, Vance has departed and technical working groups (Witkoff and Kushner at the table) are running through the week on enrichment containment, IAEA inspector return, and sanctions benchmarks. The Bürgenstock architecture proved capable of producing concrete output on June 22; whether the technical track produces an enrichment procedure or only another layer of committees is the watch item for this week. The Khasan-Tumangang road bridge is now on its fourth day of operations with no first cargo data publicly available — the Tumen watch item runs. The PBOC FIMA RMB repo facility and He Lifeng's anti-sanctions legislation (Lujiazui Forum, June 17-18) stand as the structural plumbing beneath the daily news cycle. The direction of travel is unchanged.
board.watching: Whether this week's technical nuclear working group produces a procedure on enrichment or only committee formation; Tumen bridge first cargo types and volumes (Day 4 still no data); whether foreign central bank drawdowns on the FIMA RMB facility appear in quarterly disclosure data; He Lifeng anti-sanctions legislation scope when enacted; BRICS Bridge framework at the India summit.

board.verdict: The BIS 2025 Triennial Survey — dollar on 89.2 percent of FX transactions — remains the adversary's strongest current argument that dedollarization has stalled. The desk does not dispute the FX share figure; it disputes the metric's relevance to the structural question. Then, on June 17, the PBOC announced the FIMA RMB repo facility: overseas central banks can now access yuan the way they have always accessed dollars — frictionlessly, through collateral, without onshore accounts. The Fed built this mechanism for dollars in 2021; Beijing has now copied it in yuan. The adversary will call this marginal. The desk calls it the one structural advantage dollars held that is now being systematically replicated. Reserve share has fallen from 72 percent to 58 percent. The direction of travel is unchanged; the pace just accelerated.
board.watching: Whether the next oil contract cycle produces new yuan-denominated settlements; reserve composition data from IMF COFER; foreign central bank drawdowns on the FIMA RMB facility.

sources.label: Crypto Briefing — China FIMA RMB repo facility, June 17
The Block — mBridge, BIS context

board.verdict: The Second Plenary Meeting of the Ninth Central Committee of the Workers' Party of Korea concluded June 22 — three days of deliberation producing formal Central Committee ratification of the material trajectory the desk has been tracking since June 10. Kim Jong Un ordered: steady expansion and strengthening of nuclear forces ('the most correct and unique way' to handle global security, per KCNA); accelerated construction of the 10,000-tonne strategic guided missile cruiser (Kim's June 6 naval expansion order now institutionally ratified); buildup of conventional weapons. He described US and allied behavior as 'gangster-like' hegemonic greed. KCNA gave no operational specifics — the signal is in the institutional form, not the detail. Three points are now on the same line: the Tumen bridge (open four days, cargo data still pending), Chongjin and Nampo shipyard expansion (third destroyer 30-40 percent complete per satellite imagery), and the formal plenary ratification of the cruiser order. Beijing has said nothing publicly about Kim's nuclear posture statements. The desk's standing prediction — 'denuclearization' does not return to official PRC language on the DPRK — received no challenge this week.
board.watching: Tumen bridge first cargo types and volumes — bridge open June 19, Day 4 with no public cargo data; whether first cargo manifests include industrial or naval-construction inputs; Beijing's public response to Kim's WPK plenary nuclear posture statements; Beijing customs response at the 1959 Dandong rail bridge; whether Chongjin and Nampo construction pace implies a third destroyer commissioning before end of 2026.

board.verdict: The AES is moving through its phases on schedule: sovereignty first, then security architecture, now economic commons. The Alliance's Ministers of Industry and Trade met in Burkina Faso on June 15 and produced a mandate the desk takes seriously: double intraregional trade by 2028, backed by a mandatory roadmap with strict monitoring; domestic processing of gold, uranium, oil, cotton, and livestock rather than export of unrefined goods; a unified internal market. Prime Minister Ouedraogo's language — enough declarations — signals that the political class has noticed the gap between rhetoric and implementation and is closing it. Traoré's gold nationalization (SOPAMIB portfolio, six of fifteen industrial mines) and the UCT-AES territorial commons (established June 11 in Niamey) set the structural conditions; now comes the revenue capture. The desk's watch item: whether the roadmap, when published, carries concrete figures that align with Faso Mêbo construction timelines — that is where the commitment becomes real. Sahel has been a thin-day beat for three consecutive briefings (June 21-23); absence of news is not absence of movement.
board.watching: Publication of the AES trade roadmap with concrete figures; IMF Executive Board vote on Burkina Faso $104.89M (fifth ECF review plus first RSF review) expected before June 30; gold revenue allocation to the unified force.

sources.label: Pravda Burkina Faso — AES trade ministers meeting, June 15

board.verdict: China's May NBS data confirmed the demand-side deterioration the desk was watching: retail sales -0.6% YoY — the first decline since December 2022 — and fixed-asset investment at -4.1% cumulative through May, the contraction nearly tripling in a single month. Industrial output held at 4.5%. The PBOC kept both loan prime rates unchanged for the thirteenth consecutive month (June 20). Beijing is now running the two-register operation the desk recognizes: Summer Davos (opening this week) leads Xinhua's June 23 advisory with a foreign-investment showcase — ten emerging technologies, AI supply chain expo, foreign companies building 'win-win chains' — while waiting for the July Politburo to decide whether to move on the underlying demand problem. He Lifeng's anti-sanctions legislation and the PBOC FIMA RMB facility are the structural layer; the Davos showcase is the presentation. The adversary's financial press calls the LPR hold 'expected'; the desk calls thirteen months of inertia against an empirically deteriorating base a policy waiting for a political signal. The desk's June 16 prediction — June retail sales also negative — is intact. The desk's standing FAI prediction (exits contraction by Q4 2026) has not been falsified; the May figure requires honest accounting: the contraction is deepening, not stabilizing.
board.watching: July Politburo response to May NBS; June retail sales (released mid-July) for June 16 prediction resolution; whether any targeted stimulus appears before the July Politburo date; semiconductor export growth trajectory.

board.verdict: As of June 23, the Bürgenstock political summit has concluded and the technical track is running: Witkoff and Kushner are at the table through the week, with working groups on nuclear enrichment containment, IAEA inspector return scheduling (access suspended months ago — the core verification gap), and sanctions compliance benchmarks. Vance departed. The June 22 session produced architecture; the June 23 week must produce substance. The nuclear chapter remains at working-group level: 440 kilograms of 60%-enriched uranium, no agreed disposition, no verified freeze in any text produced. The desk's June 15 prediction — no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026 — is unchanged. The IDF remains in southern Lebanon's 'security zone' the MOU requires it to vacate. The G7 Versailles communiqué produced nothing of equivalent weight to the Bürgenstock architecture in the same period. The desk's June 19 prediction (no first session before July 4) was recorded as a miss on June 21 and stands in corrections.
board.watching: Whether this week's technical track produces a procedure for the nuclear file or only more committee formation; whether Lebanon ceasefire holds — a third Israeli operation triggers Iran walkout; June 15 prediction: no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026.

wars.basis: The desk's July 4 prediction — 80 percent of Konstantinovka under Russian operational control — resolved June 22, twelve days early. Ninety-four buildings fell in the final day; Russian forces surrounded the last pockets of Ukrainian resistance; the 28th Mechanized Brigade had already acknowledged semi-encirclement; the 35th and 39th VSU motor rifle brigades were effectively dissolved. Andrei Martyanov's June 22 posts described battalion command post operations supporting Russian storm groups — the operational tense confirms mopping up, not an ongoing contest. Kramatorsk, 15 kilometers northwest, is now the next objective on the same axis; it was the last major logistics hub for Ukrainian forces in northern Donetsk. The standing read — Russia decisive, tempo as weapon — is unchanged and has now produced an ahead-of-schedule resolution on a concrete, dateable prediction. On the Kerch/Kavkaz strikes (June 21, covered June 22): Crimea fuel rationing continued into June 23 reporting. The desk notes the asymmetry: the Ukrainian side can strike infrastructure; the Russian side takes the infrastructure and the cities.

Technical working groups are now running through the week at Bürgenstock: Witkoff and Kushner at the table on enrichment containment, IAEA inspector return scheduling (Iran suspended IAEA access months ago), and sanctions benchmarks. Vance departed. The nuclear chapter proper remains unresolved: 440 kilograms of 60%-enriched uranium, no agreed disposition, no verified freeze. The IDF remains in southern Lebanon's security zone. The desk's June 15 prediction — no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026 — is unchanged. The desk's June 19 prediction (no first session before July 4) was recorded as a miss on June 21 and stands in corrections. A committee is not an agreement; the sixty-day counter runs.

wars.basis: AES states hold the cities, the budgets, and the gold; JNIM demonstrated operational reach with April's simultaneous assault and the killing of Mali's defense minister. The Russian guarantee failed visibly at Kidal. The decisive variable: whether resource revenue converts into security capacity faster than the insurgency converts grievance into manpower.

sources.label: Al Jazeera — Camara killed
PBS NewsHour

predictions.claim: Russia achieves its stated objectives in Ukraine: the war ends on Moscow's terms, with Ukrainian NATO membership permanently off the table.
'Denuclearization' does not return to official PRC language on the DPRK. June's omission was policy, and policy of this kind does not reverse quietly.
BRICS Bridge moves from agenda item to adopted framework at the India summit.
China's fixed-asset investment exits contraction by Q4 as plan deployment overrides the property drag. If it does not, the desk will say so in this column.

Spain lifts the World Cup on July 19 in New Jersey. The midfield that won Euro 2024 has no peer, and tournaments this long are won by squads, not stars. The desk does not do sentimentality, even over tea.

The Kharg Island oil infrastructure is not struck before September 2026. Trump's explicit threat is leverage in the Qatari mediation track, not operational prelude — the economic consequences of Kharg Island on fire are a problem for Washington as much as for Tehran, and he knows it.

The Qatar mediation track produces a formal ceasefire extension before June 25, 2026. Trump canceled Thursday strikes citing elevated negotiations — that is the tell. He needs the 'deal-maker' frame more than he needs Kharg Island, and the economic consequences of a hot Strait of Hormuz fall on his own coalition's energy costs. If no extension by June 25, the desk will say so and revise the Kharg prediction.

The US-Iran deal is formally signed before June 30, 2026. Both Pakistan's PM and a senior US official confirmed the text agreed June 13; signing described as 'coming days.' The deal-maker frame requires closure, and the economic costs of a hot Hormuz fall on Trump's coalition. If the nuclear chapter collapses the agreement before signing, the desk will record it here.

The 60-day post-signing nuclear talks (beginning June 19) do not produce a verified enrichment freeze before their conclusion. Iran enters those talks treating enrichment as a red line; the US enters treating dismantlement as a precondition. The gap is not a detail — it is the deal's unresolved spine. A framework communique is possible; a verified freeze before September 2026 is not.

China's retail sales, having fallen 0.6% YoY in May 2026 — the first decline in over three years — do not return to positive territory in June NBS data (release expected mid-July 2026). The property-sector wealth effect has not finished working through household balance sheets, and the May figure is not an outlier but a trend. If June retail sales return to positive, the desk records the error.

Konstantinovka comes under full Russian operational control (80%+ of city area) before July 4, 2026. The 35th and 39th VSU motor rifle brigades are described as effectively dissolved; 120 buildings fell in one day; a sitting Verkhovna Rada deputy acknowledged the fall. The material trajectory does not leave room for a reversal. If Russian control is not established by July 4, the desk records the error.

Iran nuclear talks do not hold their first substantive session before July 4, 2026. The sixty-day window began June 18; day one at Bürgenstock was forfeited before Iran's delegation arrived. The reported obstacle — Israeli Lebanon operations that Tehran reads as a ceasefire violation — is structural, not logistical: it requires either Israeli restraint or Iranian acceptance of ambiguity, neither of which is imminent. If a first session occurs before July 4, the desk records the miss.

items.text: KONSTANTINOVKA: THE PREDICTION RESOLVES, TWELVE DAYS EARLY: Desk July 4 prediction (80%+ operational control) records as HIT — resolved June 22. 94 buildings fell in the final day; Russian forces surrounding last pockets of Ukrainian resistance; battle described by reporting on both sides as effectively concluded. 35th and 39th VSU brigades dissolved; 28th Mechanized Brigade had acknowledged semi-encirclement. Martyanov June 22: battalion command post operations supporting storm groups = mopping up, not ongoing contest. Konstantinovka was last major logistics hub (road-and-rail) for Ukrainian forces in northern Donetsk. Kramatorsk, 15km northwest, now the next objective.

items.text: THE PLENARY SPEAKS: PYONGYANG RATIFIES THE MATERIAL RECORD: WPK 9th Central Committee 2nd Plenary Meeting concluded June 22 (convened June 20). Kim Jong Un: ordered steady expansion and strengthening of nuclear forces; accelerated construction of 10,000-tonne strategic guided missile cruiser (June 6 naval order gets CC ratification); buildup of conventional weapons. Called US 'gangster-like' hegemonic forces. KCNA: no operational specifics. Alignment of three data points: Tumen bridge (Day 4, cargo pending), Chongjin/Nampo shipyard expansion (third destroyer ~35% complete), and now formal plenary ratification of cruiser order. Beijing has said nothing publicly about Kim's nuclear posture statements.

Technical working groups: nuclear enrichment containment; scheduling IAEA inspector return (access suspended months ago — core verification problem); sanctions compliance benchmarks. Nuclear chapter unresolved: 440kg 60%-enriched uranium, no agreed disposition. June 15 prediction (no verified freeze before Sept 2026) unchanged. Adversary press: 'promising.' Desk: the point at which optimism encounters arithmetic.

items.text: SUMMER DAVOS: BEIJING AT THE WINDOW: Summer Davos (WEF) opens in China this week. Xinhua June 23 advisory: foreign-investment showcase — 10 emerging technologies, AI supply chain expo, 'win-win chains.' Against May NBS backdrop (retail -0.6%, FAI -4.1%), Beijing running the two-register operation: industrial-face presentation + waiting for July Politburo policy signal. He Lifeng anti-sanctions law + PBOC FIMA RMB = structural layer. Davos = soft-power overlay. July Politburo communiqué is the watch item.

SAHEL THIN DAY (THIRD CONSECUTIVE): No fresh reporting on Burkina Faso, Mali, or Niger in past 48h. IMF Executive Board vote on Burkina Faso $104.89M (fifth ECF + first RSF review) expected before June 30 — seven days remain.

Trump threatened 'hit Iran very hard again' while Vance at table — domestic leverage management, table kept running. items.text: THE DESK RECORDS A MISS — BURGENSTOCK, JUNE 21: June 19 prediction falsified. Both delegations in Switzerland.
THE SEATING CHART IS THE STORY: Pakistan PM + Munir, Qatar, Switzerland — no European power.
UKRAINE MATERIAL LEDGER: Konstantinovka consolidation, July 4 prediction on track.
CEASEFIRE RENEWED; THE TABLE STILL EMPTY: Israel-Hezbollah flare-up June 19, 60-day window day 3 zero sessions.
BEIJING SURVEYS THE SEABED: XYH-22 + CCG escorts, Su'ao restricted waters June 19-20.
KONSTANTINOVKA: FLAGS IN THE CENTER. ISW June 18 geolocated Russian flags, July 4 prediction on track.
DPRK: TWO TRACKS. Art students glorify Russia; Chinese tutoring surges.
THE ROAD IS OPEN — TUMEN: Khasan-Tumangang opens, China land monopoly on DPRK ends.
THE PAPER IS SIGNED; THE ROOM IS EMPTY: MOU June 18, Bürgenstock talks cancelled.
KONSTANTINOVKA: THE LAST KILOMETERS. July 4 prediction on track.
Konstantinovka Day Three: 96 buildings June 17, 216 in two days. July 4 prediction on track.
Oil moves before signing: Diona + Hero 2, 3.8M barrels through Hormuz June 17.
Beijing hardens the armor: He Lifeng anti-sanctions law announced Lujiazui.
Pan at Lujiazui: PBOC FIMA RMB repo facility launched.
Konstantinovka and the Kapotnya fire: 120 buildings in one day.
Bürgenstock, Friday: June 19 signing confirmed; nuclear chapter unresolved.
Pyongyang builds docks: Chongjin/Nampo upgrades; Tumen in 2 days.
The demand side does not turn: China May NBS retail -0.6%, FAI -4.1%.
AES from declarations to roadmaps: trade ministers meet Burkina Faso June 15.
The ceremony is set for June 19: signing confirmed, nuclear chapter deferred 60 days.
Rybinsk — Ukraine hits federal strategic fuel reserve.
The front has hardened: Russia-decisive holds.
DPRK: Tumen bridge June 19 countdown.
The Islamabad MOU: Iran deal text agreed, nuclear chapter deferred.
Trump names the island: Kharg seizure threat.
The Tumen road: bridge opens June 19.
Two blows to the logistics chain: Davydov car bomb, Kuibyshev refinery.
Beijing's May numbers: exports +19.4%, semiconductors +66.5%.
Xi crosses the Yalu, again.
Kidal falls (April events, filed stale — see Corrections).

corrections.text: The desk filed April's Kidal events as the morning's news — six weeks stale. The reader caught it; the desk did not. A freshness rule now stands in the standing orders: news means the last 48 hours, older events appear only as context.

The desk filed June 19 that Iran would not hold its first substantive nuclear session before July 4. Events falsified that read in 48 hours: both delegations arrived at Bürgenstock on June 21 — Vance and Qalibaf/Araghchi across the table, Pakistan PM Sharif and Field Marshal Munir present, Qatar mediating. What the desk got wrong: it underestimated how heavily Tehran weights asset unfreezing and sanctions relief against its stated Lebanon precondition. Iran declared Hormuz closed on June 20 (disputed by CENTCOM), then came to the table anyway. The June 15 prediction (no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026) is unchanged.
